title: Nanny of the Maroons Leads Resistance
description: Nanny, a Jamaican Maroon leader, helps coordinate resistance to British colonial forces and protects autonomous communities.

title: Phillis Wheatley Publishes Poetry
description: Phillis Wheatley becomes one of the first published African American poets, challenging ideas about race, intellect, and authorship.

title: Mary Prince Testifies About Slavery
description: The History of Mary Prince becomes a major first-person account of slavery by a Black woman in the British Caribbean.

title: Frederick Douglass Launches The North Star
description: Douglass creates an abolitionist newspaper amplifying anti-slavery writing and Black political thought.

title: Sojourner Truth Delivers Ain't I a Woman?
description: Sojourner Truth links abolition and women's rights, exposing how race and gender oppression intersect.

title: Harriet Tubman Leads Rescue Missions
description: Tubman leads intelligence and liberation missions during the US Civil War, including the Combahee Ferry Raid.

title: First Native American Woman Doctor Licensed in US
description: Susan La Flesche Picotte earns her medical degree and serves Native communities with a focus on public health.

title: Ida B. Wells Publishes Anti-Lynching Investigations
description: Wells documents racial terror in investigative journalism, shaping global anti-lynching activism.

title: Yuri Kochiyama Born; Future Cross-Movement Organizer
description: Kochiyama later becomes a key organizer connecting Japanese American redress, Black liberation, and anti-imperialist struggles.

title: Zora Neale Hurston's Anthropological Work Gains Reach
description: Hurston's fieldwork and writing preserve Black folklore traditions often dismissed by mainstream scholarship.

title: Mendez v. Westminster Challenges School Segregation
description: Mexican American families in California win a key desegregation case that influences later civil rights litigation.

title: Claudette Colvin Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
description: Months before the more widely known boycott moment, Claudette Colvin's resistance becomes part of legal strategy against bus segregation.

title: Dolores Huerta Co-Founds United Farm Workers
description: Huerta helps organize farmworkers for labor rights, better pay, and safer working conditions.

title: Nina Simone Releases Mississippi Goddam
description: Nina Simone turns performance into protest, using music to confront racist violence in the US.

title: American Indian Movement Founded
description: AIM is founded to address police brutality, treaty rights, sovereignty, and conditions in Native communities.

title: The Combahee River Collective Statement
description: Black feminist organizers articulate an intersectional political framework centered on those most marginalized.

title: Rigoberta Menchu's Testimonio Goes Global
description: The life narrative of Rigoberta Menchu highlights Indigenous rights struggles in Guatemala for international audiences.

title: Dr. Flossie Wong-Staal Helps Map HIV
description: Virologist Flossie Wong-Staal's work on HIV genetics advances understanding and testing in the AIDS crisis.

title: Disability Rights Movement Wins ADA
description: Activists secure the Americans with Disabilities Act, reshaping access and anti-discrimination law.

title: Wangari Maathai Wins Nobel Peace Prize
description: Maathai is recognized for linking environmental restoration, women's leadership, and democratic rights in Kenya.

title: Tarana Burke's Me Too Movement Expands
description: Tarana Burke's long-running survivor-centered framework reaches global scale and reshapes discussions of accountability.

title: Land Back and Language Revitalization Campaigns Grow
description: Indigenous-led movements around land stewardship and language renewal gain broader institutional support.
